title: Pelargonium punctatum
intro: Pelargonium punctatum, also known as the spotted geranium, is a perennial plant species in the Geraniaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, particularly in treating digestive issues and fever, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Pelargonium punctatum include flavonoids, such as luteolin and quercetin, as well as phenolic acids like caffeic acid and rosmarinic acid. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used in traditional medicine to alleviate symptoms of colds, flu, and digestive disorders.
taxonomy: Pelargonium punctatum is classified as a species of flowering plant within the family Geraniaceae. This classification places it within the order Geraniales. The family Geraniaceae also includes other plants commonly known as geraniums or pelargoniums. Pelargonium punctatum is native to Australia and is often found in the western regions. The classification of P. punctatum reflects its botanical relationship to other plants within the Geraniaceae family.
